text: The warehouse had been empty for eleven years. Mara knew this because she had counted every winter from the window of the apartment across the street, watching pigeons claim the loading dock and weeds split the asphalt. When the lights came on one Tuesday night in October, she did not call the police. She put on her coat instead. This was the difference between Mara and most people: she moved toward the thing that did not make sense, rather than away from it. The door was unlocked. Inside, someone had arranged forty-seven folding chairs in a circle, and on each chair sat a single white envelope with a name written on it. One of them was hers.
